volumeTitle: Persians. Seven against Thebes. Suppliants. Prometheus Bound
label: Persians (Section 623 - Section 755)

sections.label: Section 623
sections.original: βασίλεια γύναι , πρέσβος Πέρσαις ,
sections.translation: Royal lady , august queen of the Persians , pour these libations down to the chambers of the earth ,

sections.label: Section 625
sections.original: ἡμεῖς θ ὕμνοις αἰτησόμεθα
sections.translation: while we , in solemn chant , beseech the guides of the dead beneath the earth to be gracious to our prayers . O holy divinities of the nether world , Earth and Hermes , and you , Lord of the dead ,

sections.label: Section 630
sections.original: πέμψατ ἔνερθεν ψυχὴν ἐς φῶς :
sections.translation: send up to the light the spirit from below ; for if , beyond our prayers , he knows any further remedy for our distress , he alone of mortals can declare how to bring it to accomplishment .

sections.label: Section 1.633
sections.original: ἦ ῥ ἀίει μου [*] μακαρίτας
sections.translation: Does our sainted and godlike king hear me as I utter ,

sections.label: Section 1.635
sections.original: βάρβαρ σαφηνῆ
sections.translation: in obscure barbaric speech , my dismal and dolorous cries ? Or must I shout aloud the utter misery of my anguish so that it pierces the earth ? Does he hear me from below ?

sections.label: Section 1.640
sections.original: ἀλλὰ σύ μοι Γᾶ τε καὶ ἄλλοι
sections.translation: O Earth , and you other rulers of those who dwell in the nether world , ensure , I implore , that the glorious spirit , the god of the Persians , whom Susa bore , may quit his abode .

sections.label: Section 1.645
sections.original: πέμπετε [*] δ ἄνω τὸν [*] οἷον οὔπω
sections.translation: Send to the upper world him the likes of whom the Persian earth has never entombed .

sections.label: Section 2.647
sections.original: ἦ φίλος ἁνήρ , [*] [ ἦ [*] ] φίλος ὄχθος :
sections.translation: Beloved indeed was the hero , beloved is his burial mound ; beloved are the qualities that lie buried there . O Aidoneus ,

sections.label: Section 2.650
sections.original: ὸς [*] ἀνείης , Ἀιδωνεύς ,
sections.translation: Aidoneus , who convey shades to the upper air , permit our divine lord Darius to come forth !

sections.label: Section 2.652
sections.original: οὐδὲ [*] γὰρ ἄνδρας ποτ ἀπώλλυ [*]
sections.translation: For since he did not ever cause the destruction of his people by senseless and ruinous wars , he bore the name of divine counsellor

sections.label: Section 2.655
sections.original: σκετο Πέρσαις , θεομήστωρ δ
sections.translation: to the Persians ; and a divine counsellor he was , since he guided his people well .

sections.label: Section 3.657
sections.original: βαλήν , [*] ἀρχαῖος βαλήν ,
sections.translation: King , our king of old , come forth , draw near ! Rise to the barrow ’ s topmost point ,

sections.label: Section 3.660
sections.original: κροκόβαπτον ποδὸς εὔμαριν ἀείρων ,
sections.translation: lift your saffron-dyed sandal , display the crest of your royal tiara ! Come forth , O blameless father Darius .

sections.label: Section 3.664
sections.original: ὅπως αἰανῆ κλύῃς
sections.translation: That you may hear

sections.label: Section 3.665
sections.original: νέα τ ἄχη ,
sections.translation: pitiable and unheard-of sorrows , O Lord of our lord , appear ! For a gloom , like that of Styx , hovers over us , since all the youth of the land

sections.label: Section 3.670
sections.original: κατὰ πᾶσ ὄλωλεν .
sections.translation: is now utterly destroyed . Come forth , O blameless father Darius !

sections.label: Section 673
sections.original: ὦ πολύκλαυτε φίλοισι θανών ,
sections.translation: Alas , alas ! You whose death your friends bewailed with bitter tears ,

sections.label: Section 675
sections.original: † τί τάδε δυνάτα [*] δυνάτα
sections.translation: why , my king , my king , why is it that our land has lost all its three-tiered galleys ,

sections.label: Section 680
sections.original: νᾶες ἄναες ἄναες .
sections.translation: ships that are no more , no more ?

sections.label: Section 681
sections.original: ὦ πιστὰ πιστῶν ἥλικές θ ἥβης ἐμῆς
sections.translation: O most faithful of the faithful , comrades of my youth , aged Persians , what is it that is troubling the state ? The earth groans and is furrowed by the stamp of men . As I behold my wife by my tomb ,

sections.label: Section 685
sections.original: ταρβῶ , χοὰς δὲ πρευμενὴς ἐδεξάμην .
sections.translation: I feel alarm , and I accept her libations in kindly mood ; while you , standing near my tomb , make lament , and with shrill cries that summon the spirits of the dead , invoke me piteously . Not easy is the path out of the tomb , for this reason above all , that the gods beneath the earth

sections.label: Section 690
sections.original: λαβεῖν ἀμείνους εἰσὶν ἢ μεθιέναι .
sections.translation: are readier to seize than to release . Nevertheless , since I have obtained dominion among them , I have come . But make haste , so that I may not incur blame regarding the time of my sojourn . What is this unexpected ill that weighs the Persians down ?

sections.label: Section 1.694
sections.original: σέβομαι μὲν προσιδέσθαι ,
sections.translation: I shrink in awe from gazing upon you .

sections.label: Section 1.695
sections.original: σέβομαι δ ἀντία λέξαι
sections.translation: I shrink in awe from speaking in your presence by reason of my former dread of you .

sections.label: Section 1.697
sections.original: ἀλλ ἐπεὶ κάτωθεν ἦλθον σοῖς γόοις πεπεισμένος , [*]
sections.translation: Since , in compliance with your moanings , I have come from the world below , lay aside your awe of me ; make your tale not long , but brief ; speak out and deliver your story in its entirety .

sections.label: Section 1.700
sections.original: δίεμαι μὲν χαρίσασθαι ,
sections.translation: I fear to do your bidding ; I fear to speak in your presence and deliver to those I love news hard to utter .

sections.label: Section 703
sections.original: ἀλλ ἐπεὶ δέος παλαιὸν σοὶ φρενῶν ἀνθίσταται ,
sections.translation: Since dread long ingrained in your mind restrains you ,

sections.label: Section 705
sections.original: κλαυμάτων λήξασα τῶνδε καὶ γόων σαφές τί μοι
sections.translation: cease , noble woman , venerable partner of my bed , from your tears and laments , speak to me with all frankness . Afflictions ordained for human life must , we know , befall mankind . For many calamities from the sea , many from the land , arise to mortal men if their span of life is extended far .

sections.label: Section 709
sections.original: ὦ βροτῶν πάντων ὑπερσχὼν ὄλβον εὐτυχεῖ πότμῳ , [*]
sections.translation: O you who in prosperity surpassed all mortal men by your happy destiny ,

sections.label: Section 710
sections.original: ὡς ἕως τ ἔλευσσες αὐγὰς ἡλίου ζηλωτὸς ὢν
sections.translation: since , so long as you gazed upon the beams of the sun , you lived a life of felicity , envied of all , in Persian eyes a god , so now too I consider you fortunate in that you died before you beheld the depth of our calamities . The whole tale , O Darius , you will hear in brief space of time : the power of Persia is ruined almost utterly .

sections.label: Section 715
sections.original: τίνι τρόπῳ ; λοιμοῦ τις ἦλθε σκηπτός , ἢ στάσις πόλει ;
sections.translation: How did it happen ? Did some stroke of pestilence or factional strife come upon the State ?

sections.label: Section 716
sections.original: οὐδαμῶς : ἀλλ ἀμφ Ἀθήνας πᾶς κατέφθαρται στρατός .
sections.translation: Neither ; but near Athens our whole host has been brought to ruin .

sections.label: Section 717
sections.original: τίς δ ἐμῶν ἐκεῖσε παίδων ἐστρατηλάτει ; φράσον .
sections.translation: Tell me , what son of mine led our army there ?

sections.label: Section 718
sections.original: θούριος Ξέρξης , κενώσας πᾶσαν ἠπείρου πλάκα .
sections.translation: Impetuous Xerxes , depopulating the whole surface of the continent .

sections.label: Section 719
sections.original: πεζὸς ἢ ναύτης δὲ πεῖραν τήνδ ἐμώρανεν τάλας ;
sections.translation: Was it by land or sea that he made this mad expedition , the reckless man ?

sections.label: Section 720
sections.original: ἀμφότερα : διπλοῦν μέτωπον ἦν δυοῖν [*] στρατευμάτοιν . [*]
sections.translation: By both . There was a twofold front of double armies .

sections.label: Section 721
sections.original: πῶς δὲ καὶ στρατὸς τοσόσδε [*] πεζὸς ἤνυσεν περᾶν ;
sections.translation: But how was it that so vast a land force won a passage to the farther shore ?

sections.label: Section 722
sections.original: μηχαναῖς ἔζευξεν Ἕλλης πορθμόν , ὥστ ἔχειν πόρον .
sections.translation: By a clever device he yoked the Hellespont so as to gain a passage .

sections.label: Section 723
sections.original: καὶ τόδ ἐξέπραξεν , ὥστε Βόσπορον κλῇσαι μέγαν ;
sections.translation: What ! Did he succeed in closing the mighty Bosporus ?

sections.label: Section 724
sections.original: ὧδ [*] ἔχει : γνώμης δέ πού τις δαιμόνων ξυνήψατο .
sections.translation: Yes indeed . One of the divine powers must have assisted him in his purpose .

sections.label: Section 725
sections.original: φεῦ , μέγας τις ἦλθε δαίμων , ὥστε μὴ φρονεῖν καλῶς .
sections.translation: Alas ! Some mighty power came upon him so that he was not able to think clearly .

sections.label: Section 726
sections.original: ὡς ἰδεῖν τέλος πάρεστιν οἷον ἤνυσεν κακόν .
sections.translation: Yes , since we can see the outcome , what ruin he wrought .

sections.label: Section 727
sections.original: καὶ τί δὴ πράξασιν αὐτοῖς ὧδ ἐπιστενάζετε ;
sections.translation: And how then did they fare that you now lament them ?

sections.label: Section 728
sections.original: ναυτικὸς στρατὸς κακωθεὶς πεζὸν ὤλεσε στρατόν .
sections.translation: Disaster to the naval force brought ruin to the force on land .

sections.label: Section 729
sections.original: ὧδε παμπήδην δὲ λαὸς πᾶς κατέφθαρται δορί ;
sections.translation: And did the whole army utterly perish by the spear ?

sections.label: Section 730
sections.original: πρὸς τάδ ὡς Σούσων [*] μὲν [*] ἄστυ πᾶν κενανδρίαν στένειν .
sections.translation: Yes , and it is for this reason that the whole city of Susa groans at its desolation .

sections.label: Section 731
sections.original: ὦ πόποι κεδνῆς ἀρωγῆς κἀπικουρίας στρατοῦ .
sections.translation: Alas for the loss of our warriors ’ valiant force and defence !

sections.label: Section 732
sections.original: Βακτρίων δ ἔρρει πανώλης δῆμος , οὐδέ [*] τις γέρων .
sections.translation: And the host of the Bactrians is lost , wholly destroyed : not even an old man is left .

sections.label: Section 733
sections.original: ὦ μέλεος , οἵαν ἄρ ἥβην ξυμμάχων ἀπώλεσεν .
sections.translation: Unhappy man , since he has brought to ruin the youth of our allies .

sections.label: Section 734
sections.original: μονάδα δὲ Ξέρξην ἔρημόν φασιν οὐ πολλῶν μέτα —
sections.translation: But Xerxes , alone and forlorn , with scanty train , they say —

sections.label: Section 735
sections.original: πῶς τε δὴ καὶ ποῖ τελευτᾶν ; ἔστι τις σωτηρία ;
sections.translation: Met his end , how , tell me , and where ? Of his safety is there any hope ?

sections.label: Section 736
sections.original: ἄσμενον μολεῖν γέφυραν γαῖν [*] δυοῖν [*] ζευκτηρίαν .
sections.translation: To his joy he reached the bridge yoking the two continents .

sections.label: Section 737
sections.original: καὶ πρὸς ἤπειρον σεσῶσθαι τήνδε , τοῦτ ἐτήτυμον ;
sections.translation: And reached our continent in safety ? Is this certain ?

sections.label: Section 738
sections.original: ναί : λόγος κρατεῖ σαφηνὴς τοῦτό γ , οὐδ [*] ἔνι στάσις .
sections.translation: Yes , a proven report establishes this . Doubt there is none .

sections.label: Section 739
sections.original: φεῦ , ταχεῖά γ ἦλθε χρησμῶν πρᾶξις , ἐς δὲ παῖδ ἐμὸν
sections.translation: Alas ! The fulfilment of the oracles has indeed come swiftly , and it is my son upon whom

sections.label: Section 740
sections.original: Ζεὺς ἐπέσκηψεν [*] τελευτὴν θεσφάτων : ἐγὼ δέ που
sections.translation: Zeus has caused their issue to descend . Yet I was confident that , only after long lapse of time , the gods would in some way bring them to accomplishment ; nevertheless , when man hastens to his own undoing , the god too participates with him . A fountain of misfortune has now , I think , been discovered for all I love . A son of mine it was who , in his ignorance , brought these things to pass through youthful recklessness ;

sections.label: Section 745
sections.original: ὅστις Ἑλλήσποντον ἱρὸν δοῦλον ὣς δεσμώμασιν
sections.translation: for he conceived the hope that he could by shackles , as if it were a slave , restrain the current of the sacred Hellespont , the Bosporus , a stream divine ; he set himself to fashion a roadway of a new type , and , by casting upon it hammer-wrought fetters , made a spacious causeway for his mighty host . Mortal though he was , he thought in his folly that he would gain the mastery of all the gods ,

sections.label: Section 750
sections.original: καὶ Ποσειδῶνος κρατήσειν . πῶς τάδ οὐ νόσος [*] φρενῶν
sections.translation: yes , even over Poseidon . Must this not have been a disease of the soul that possessed my son ? I fear that the plenteous treasure amassed by my toil may become the prey of the spoiler .

sections.label: Section 753
sections.original: ταῦτά τοι [*] κακοῖς ὁμιλῶν ἀνδράσιν διδάσκεται
sections.translation: This lesson impetuous Xerxes learned through conversation with evil men . For they kept telling him that , whereas you

sections.label: Section 755
sections.original: πλοῦτον ἐκτήσω ξὺν αἰχμῇ , τὸν δ ἀνανδρίας ὕπο
sections.translation: won plentiful treasure for your children by your spear , he , on his part , through lack of manly spirit , played the warrior at home and did not increase his father ’ s wealth . Hearing such taunts many a time from evil counsellors , he planned this expedition and army against Hellas .
